explain.rule: The second conditional is for imaginary, hypothetical, or unlikely situations in the present or future. Structure: If + past simple, would + base verb. Even though the if-clause uses past simple tense, it refers to the PRESENT or FUTURE — it is not about the past. It just imagines a different reality from now. Compare: First conditional (If it rains, I'll cancel) = real and possible. Second conditional (If I were a bird, I would fly) = imaginary or very unlikely.
explain.why_matters: Second conditional lets you talk about hypothetical situations, daydreams, giving advice (If I were you, I would...), and polite requests. It is essential for expressing wishes, dreams, and opinions about how things could be different.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew conditional mood uses אילו/לו + past for hypothetical situations: 'אילו הייתי ציפור, הייתי עפה'. This maps closely to English second conditional. The key difference: in English, 'were' is used for ALL subjects in formal/written English (If I were you, If she were here) even though 'was' is also acceptable in informal speech. In everyday speech 'If I was you' is heard but 'If I were you' is grammatically preferred.
explain.tip: Second conditional = imaginary present/future. The past tense in the if-clause is NOT about the past — it is a grammatical trick to show we are imagining something unreal. Also: 'I were' (not 'I was') in the if-clause is more correct in formal English: 'If I were you, I would apologise.' Special use: giving advice always uses second conditional — 'If I were you, I'd...'

comparison.example: If it rains, I'll stay home. (It might rain)
If I had a million dollars, I'd travel the world. (I don't have a million)

good_examples.sentence: If I had more time, I would learn to play the piano.
good_examples.audio_text: If I had more time, I would learn to play the piano.
good_examples.note: Imaginary: I don't have more time now

good_examples.sentence: She would be happier if she changed her job.
good_examples.audio_text: She would be happier if she changed her job.
good_examples.note: Hypothetical advice — she hasn't changed jobs

good_examples.sentence: If I were you, I would talk to him directly.
good_examples.audio_text: If I were you, I would talk to him directly.
good_examples.note: Giving advice — I am not you (imaginary)

good_examples.sentence: What would you do if you won the lottery?
good_examples.audio_text: What would you do if you won the lottery?

good_examples.sentence: If he spoke better English, he'd get the job.
good_examples.audio_text: If he spoke better English, he'd get the job.
good_examples.note: Imaginary: he doesn't speak better English now

good_examples.sentence: I'd move to Italy if I could work remotely.
good_examples.audio_text: I'd move to Italy if I could work remotely.

good_examples.sentence: If I were the manager, I'd change a few things.
good_examples.audio_text: If I were the manager, I'd change a few things.
good_examples.note: Hypothetical role — I am not the manager

good_examples.sentence: Would you accept the offer if they gave it to you?
good_examples.audio_text: Would you accept the offer if they gave it to you?
good_examples.note: Hypothetical question about a possible scenario

good_examples.sentence: If she didn't work so hard, she'd have more time for herself.
good_examples.audio_text: If she didn't work so hard, she'd have more time for herself.

good_examples.sentence: Life would be so boring if everything was perfect.
good_examples.audio_text: Life would be so boring if everything was perfect.

bad_examples.wrong: If I would have more time, I'd learn piano.
bad_examples.correct: If I had more time, I'd learn piano.
bad_examples.reason: NEVER use would in the if-clause. Use past simple: if I had.

bad_examples.wrong: If I were you, I will talk to him.
bad_examples.correct: If I were you, I would talk to him.
bad_examples.reason: Second conditional result clause uses would, not will. Will = first conditional.

bad_examples.wrong: What would you do if you will win the lottery?
bad_examples.correct: What would you do if you won the lottery?
bad_examples.reason: If-clause: past simple (won), not will. This is second conditional.

bad_examples.wrong: She would be happy if she would change her job.
bad_examples.correct: She would be happy if she changed her job.
bad_examples.reason: Only ONE would — in the result clause. The if-clause uses past simple.

bad_examples.wrong: If I am you, I would apologise.
bad_examples.correct: If I were you, I would apologise.
bad_examples.reason: Second conditional if-clause with be: use 'were' for all subjects, not 'am/is/are'.

exercises.question: If I ___ (have) more money, I ___ (travel) more.
exercises.hint: Imaginary present: if-clause = past simple, result = would
exercises.explanation: If I had (past simple) + would travel (result).
exercises.hebrew_note: מצב דמיוני בהווה/עתיד: if + עבר פשוט + would

exercises.question: If I ___ (be) you, I ___ (apologise) immediately.
exercises.hint: Giving advice: If I were you... Use were (not was) for I.
exercises.explanation: If I were you (were for all subjects in formal English) + would apologise.

exercises.question: She ___ (get) the job if she ___ (speak) better English.
exercises.hint: Imaginary: she doesn't speak better English now
exercises.explanation: would get (result) + if she spoke (if-clause past simple).

exercises.question: What ___ you ___ (do) if you ___ (win) the lottery?
exercises.hint: Hypothetical question — past simple in if-clause
exercises.explanation: What would you do (result) + if you won (past simple).

exercises.question: If he ___ (not / be) so stubborn, they ___ (not / argue) so much.
exercises.hint: Negative imaginary — if he weren't = if he was less stubborn
exercises.explanation: weren't (negative past of be) + wouldn't argue (negative result).

exercises.question: I ___ (move) abroad if I ___ (can) work remotely.
exercises.hint: Dream/wish about current situation
exercises.explanation: would move (result) + if I could (past of can).

exercises.question: ___ you ___ (accept) the job if they ___ (offer) it to you?
exercises.hint: Hypothetical question — would in question, past simple in if-clause
exercises.explanation: Would you accept (result question) + if they offered (past simple).

exercises.question: If I ___ (know) her number, I ___ (call) her.
exercises.hint: Imaginary: I don't know her number
exercises.explanation: knew (past simple) + would call (result).

exercises.question: Life ___ (be) easier if everyone ___ (speak) the same language.
exercises.hint: General hypothetical — imaginary world
exercises.explanation: would be (result) + if everyone spoke (past simple).

exercises.question: If she ___ (not / work) so hard, she ___ (have) more time for herself.
exercises.hint: Imaginary change to current situation
exercises.explanation: didn't work (negative past simple) + would have (result).

exercises.question: Where ___ you ___ (go) if you ___ (can) live anywhere?
exercises.hint: Dream question — could = past of can for hypothetical
exercises.explanation: Where would you go (result) + if you could (past of can).

exercises.question: If he ___ (be) taller, he ___ (become) a professional basketball player.
exercises.hint: Imaginary physical attribute
exercises.explanation: If he were taller (past of be) + would become (result).

exercises.question: If I ___ the answer, I would tell you.
exercises.hint: Imaginary: I don't know. If-clause = past simple.
exercises.explanation: knew = past simple in imaginary if-clause.

exercises.question: Choose the correct second conditional:
exercises.answer: If she had more experience, she'd get the job.
exercises.hint: Past simple in if-clause + would in result
exercises.explanation: had (past simple) + she'd = she would. Correct second conditional.
distractors: If she has more experience, she'd get the job.
If she would have more experience, she'd get the job.
If she had more experience, she will get the job.

exercises.question: If I ___ you, I'd apologise right away.
exercises.hint: If I were you = giving advice. Use were not was.
exercises.explanation: If I were you — were is used for all subjects in second conditional with be.

exercises.question: What is the difference between these two sentences? (1) If it rains, I'll stay home. (2) If it rained, I'd stay home.
exercises.answer: Sentence 1 = real possibility. Sentence 2 = unlikely or hypothetical.
exercises.hint: First vs second conditional
exercises.explanation: will = first conditional (real). would = second conditional (hypothetical/unlikely).
distractors: Both sentences mean exactly the same thing.
Sentence 1 is about the past, sentence 2 is about the future.
Sentence 2 is more polite than sentence 1.

exercises.question: She ___ if she had the opportunity.
exercises.hint: Result clause of second conditional
exercises.explanation: would + base verb for the result in second conditional.

exercises.question: If I had wings, I ___ everywhere.
exercises.hint: Imaginary situation → result = would + base verb
exercises.explanation: would fly — imaginary result.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'If I would have more time, I'd exercise more.'
exercises.answer: If I had more time, I'd exercise more.
exercises.hint: Would is forbidden in the if-clause
exercises.explanation: NEVER use would in the if-clause. Past simple: if I had.
exercises.hebrew_note: would אסור אחרי if ב-second conditional

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'If I were you, I will talk to her.'
exercises.answer: If I were you, I would talk to her.
exercises.hint: Second conditional result → would or will?
exercises.explanation: Second conditional = would. Will = first conditional (real).

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'What would you do if you will win the lottery?'
exercises.answer: What would you do if you won the lottery?
exercises.hint: If-clause: past simple, not will
exercises.explanation: won (past simple) in if-clause. Not will win.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'If I am you, I would be more careful.'
exercises.answer: If I were you, I would be more careful.
exercises.hint: Second conditional with be → use were for all subjects
exercises.explanation: If I were you — were, not am. Formal rule for second conditional.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: If I had more time, I would read more.
exercises.hint: If + past simple + would + base verb
exercises.explanation: If I had (past simple) + I would read (result).

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: If I were you, I would apologise.
exercises.hint: Giving advice: If I were you, I would...
exercises.explanation: If I were you (were for I in second conditional) + I would apologise.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: What would you do if you lost your job?
exercises.hint: Hypothetical question: What would you do + if + past simple
exercises.explanation: What would you do (result question) + if you lost (past simple).

speak.sentence: If I had more time, I would definitely learn to cook properly.
chunks: I would definitely learn to cook properly.

speak.sentence: What would you do if you could live anywhere in the world?
chunks: if you could live anywhere in the world?

speak.sentence: If I were you, I'd talk to the manager about it.
chunks: I'd talk to the manager about it.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Were' = WUR. 'Manager' = MAN-ih-jer. 'I'd' = eyed — I would contracted.

speak.sentence: She would be so much happier if she changed her job.
chunks: She would be so much happier

speak.sentence: If I knew the answer, I'd tell you straight away.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Knew' = NYOO — silent K. 'Straight away' = immediately. STRAYT-uh-WAY.

speak.sentence: Would you accept the offer if the salary were higher?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Accept' = ak-SEPT — stress on second. 'Salary' = SAL-er-ee — three syllables.

speak.sentence: Life would be a lot easier if we didn't have to work so hard.
chunks: Life would be a lot easier
if we didn't have to work so hard.

speak.sentence: If I could speak every language in the world, I'd never stop travelling.
chunks: If I could speak every language in the world,
